title: A Day at St Kilda Beach
passage:
Emma and her dad walked along the St Kilda pier on a sunny Saturday morning. The seagulls squawked loudly above them, hoping for a chip or two.
It was Emma's first time on the pier, and she felt a little nervous looking at the water below. Her dad held her hand and pointed to the little penguins hiding under the boardwalk.
"They're so tiny!" Emma whispered. She forgot about being nervous and smiled widely. They bought ice cream from a van near the beach and sat on the sand, watching the waves roll in.
question: Why did Emma feel nervous at the start of the story?

choices.text: She lost her hat in the wind
It was her first time on the pier
She did not like ice cream

explanation: The story says it was Emma's first time on the pier.
hint: Look in the second paragraph for how Emma felt.

explanation: Seeing the penguins made Emma smile and forget her nerves.

title: Trams of Melbourne
passage:
Melbourne is famous for its trams. These big vehicles travel along tracks in the middle of the road. The number 96 tram goes from St Kilda Beach all the way to East Brunswick.
Inside the tram, passengers can hold onto poles or sit on green seats. A bell rings when the tram is about to stop. Drivers must be careful because cars and bikes share the road with trams.
Many tourists ride the free City Circle tram to see famous buildings like Flinders Street Station and Federation Square.
question: What is the main topic of this text?

choices.text: How to drive a car in Melbourne
Melbourne's trams and how they work
Why seagulls live near the beach

explanation: The whole text is about Melbourne's trams.
hint: What does every paragraph talk about?

title: The Wombat in the Garden
passage:
Last Tuesday, Mrs Chen found a wombat digging near her vegetable garden in Fitzroy. The wombat had round ears, sharp claws, and a grumpy face.
Mrs Chen called a wildlife rescue group. A kind volunteer named Jake arrived with a big cage and some fresh carrots. He explained that wombats sometimes wander into suburbs looking for food.
Together, they gently guided the wombat into the cage. Jake took it to a safe bushland area far from the busy streets. Mrs Chen waved goodbye and hoped the wombat would find a cosy burrow.
question: Who helped Mrs Chen with the wombat?
explanation: Jake from the wildlife rescue group came to help.
hint: Look for the person who arrived with a cage.

title: Should Schools Have Gardens?
passage:
Every school should have a vegetable garden. Gardens teach students where food comes from and how plants grow. Children learn patience as they wait for seeds to sprout.
School gardens also help the environment. Plants clean the air and attract helpful insects like bees. Students can even eat the vegetables they grow, which is healthy and fun.
Some people say gardens take too much time, but even a small garden can make a big difference. With just thirty minutes a week, a class can care for tomatoes, lettuce, and herbs.
question: What is the writer trying to do in this text?

choices.text: Tell a funny story about bees
Persuade readers that schools should have gardens
Describe a trip to the supermarket

explanation: The writer gives reasons why every school should have a garden.
hint: Look at the first sentence — what does the writer believe?

title: The MCG
passage:
The Melbourne Cricket Ground, or MCG, is one of the largest stadiums in the world. It can hold over 100,000 people. On big game days, the stands are packed with cheering fans wearing team colours.
The MCG is not just for cricket. Australian Rules Football matches are played there too. Families bring picnics and wave scarves when their team scores a goal.
The stadium also has a sports museum where visitors can learn about famous athletes and see old trophies. Many Melbourne families visit the MCG at least once a year.
question: Which sports are mentioned in the text?
explanation: The text mentions cricket and Australian Rules Football.
hint: Check the second paragraph.

title: Rainy Day Inside
passage:
Liam stared out the window at the grey Melbourne sky. Rain tapped on the roof like a drum. "I'm bored," he sighed.
His mum smiled and pulled out a board game from the cupboard. "Let's have a tournament," she said. They played three games of Scrabble, and Liam learned two new words: "quartz" and "jovial."
By afternoon, the sun peeked through the clouds. Liam and his mum put on gumboots and jumped in puddles on the footpath. It turned out to be the best rainy day ever.
question: How did Liam's feelings change during the story?

choices.text: He was happy, then sad, then angry
He was bored, then had fun, and ended up happy
He was scared of the rain the whole time
He stayed bored from start to finish

explanation: Liam started bored, enjoyed games with his mum, and had the best rainy day.
hint: Compare how Liam felt at the start and the end.

title: The Penguin Parade
passage:
At Phillip Island, just outside Melbourne, something amazing happens every evening. As the sun sets, tiny little penguins waddle out of the sea and cross the sand to their burrows.
These are the smallest penguins in the world. They spend their days swimming and catching fish. At night, they return home to rest and feed their chicks.
Visitors sit quietly and watch the penguins parade up the beach. People must not take photos, because the bright flashes can hurt the penguins' eyes.
question: When do the little penguins come out of the sea?
explanation: The text says as the sun sets, the penguins waddle out of the sea.
hint: Read the first paragraph about the evening.

title: Making Anzac Biscuits
passage:
Anzac biscuits are a famous Australian treat. To make them, you need oats, flour, sugar, butter, golden syrup, and coconut.
First, mix the dry ingredients in a big bowl. Next, melt the butter and golden syrup in a saucepan. Then pour the warm mixture into the bowl and stir well.
Roll the dough into small balls and place them on a baking tray. Finally, bake them in the oven until they are golden brown. Let them cool before you eat them — they will be hot!
question: What kind of text is this?
explanation: The text gives step-by-step instructions for making biscuits, so it is a recipe.
hint: Notice the words First, Next, Then, Finally.

title: Lost at the Market
passage:
Mia loved going to the Queen Victoria Market with her grandma. The market was full of colourful fruit, fresh bread, and people calling out prices.
One busy morning, Mia let go of her grandma's hand to look at some shiny red apples. When she turned around, her grandma was gone! Mia's heart began to pound.
She remembered her grandma's advice: "If you ever get lost, find someone who works at a stall." Mia walked to a friendly baker, who helped her call out a message. Soon, her grandma came rushing over and hugged her tight.
question: Where does this story take place?
explanation: The story is set at the Queen Victoria Market.
hint: Look at the first sentence.

title: The Helpful Robot
passage:
Beep the robot lived in a busy classroom. Every morning, Beep rolled around handing out pencils and books to the students.
One day, the teacher dropped a box of crayons all over the floor. The crayons rolled everywhere! Beep zoomed about, scooping them up with its gentle arms.
The students cheered and clapped. "Thank you, Beep!" they shouted. Beep's lights blinked happily. It loved helping its friends at school.
question: What is Beep?
explanation: The first sentence says Beep is a robot.
hint: Read the very first line.

title: Australian Animals at Night
passage:
Many Australian animals are nocturnal. This means they sleep during the day and come out at night.
The sugar glider is one example. It has large eyes that help it see in the dark, and flaps of skin that let it glide between trees. Possums are also active at night, searching for fruit and leaves in city gardens.
When the sun comes up, these animals find a safe, cosy place to sleep. Being awake at night helps them stay cool and hide from animals that hunt during the day.
question: What does 'nocturnal' mean?

choices.text: Sleeping at night and awake in the day
Sleeping in the day and awake at night

explanation: The text explains nocturnal animals sleep during the day and come out at night.
hint: The meaning is given in the first paragraph.

title: The Big Footy Match
passage:
Noah pulled on his team's scarf and hurried into the stadium with his uncle. The crowd roared as the players ran onto the field.
Noah's team was losing by six points. He clapped and cheered as loud as he could. In the last minute, his favourite player kicked a goal right between the posts!
The whole crowd jumped up, cheering. Noah's team had won by just one point. He would remember this exciting day for a long time.
question: How was Noah feeling when his team kicked the winning goal?
explanation: Noah jumped up cheering and called it an exciting day, showing he was excited.
hint: Look at how Noah and the crowd reacted.

title: Recycling at Home
passage:
Recycling helps keep our planet clean. When we recycle, old things are made into new things instead of going to the rubbish tip.
At home, you can sort your rubbish. Paper, glass, and plastic bottles can go into the recycling bin. Food scraps can be turned into compost for the garden.
Every small action helps. If everyone recycles a little, together we can save trees, water, and energy.
question: What is the main idea of this text?

choices.text: Recycling helps keep the planet clean

explanation: The text is mainly about how recycling helps keep our planet clean.
hint: Look at the first sentence for the big idea.

title: The Silent Ninja
passage:
Kenji was training to become a ninja. Every morning, before the sun rose, he practised moving without making a sound.
His teacher, Master Aki, set him a test. "Cross the wooden floor without it creaking," she said. The old boards squeaked at the smallest step.
Kenji breathed slowly and placed each foot near the edges of the boards, where they were strongest. Step by step, he crossed the floor in complete silence. Master Aki smiled. "You move like a shadow now," she said proudly.
question: What was Kenji training to become?
explanation: The first sentence says Kenji was training to become a ninja.
hint: Read the very first line.

title: The Big Soccer Goal
passage:
Priya loved playing soccer for her local team, the Brunswick Bolts. On Saturday, they played in the grand final.
The score was tied with only two minutes left. Priya's coach called out, "Pass the ball and look for space!" Priya dribbled past two players and spotted her teammate, Zoe, near the goal.
With a strong kick, Priya passed the ball to Zoe, who scored the winning goal. The whole team cheered and hugged. Priya learned that working together was even better than scoring by herself.
question: What is the name of Priya's soccer team?
explanation: The first paragraph says Priya played for the Brunswick Bolts.
hint: Look in the first paragraph for the team's name.

explanation: Priya passed the ball to Zoe, who scored the winning goal.
hint: Read the third paragraph carefully.

title: The Big Soccer Goal
passage:
Priya loved playing soccer for her local team, the Brunswick Bolts. On Saturday, they played in the grand final.
The score was tied with only two minutes left. Priya's coach called out, "Pass the ball and look for space!" Priya dribbled past two players and spotted her teammate, Zoe, near the goal.
With a strong kick, Priya passed the ball to Zoe, who scored the winning goal. The whole team cheered and hugged. Priya learned that working together was even better than scoring by herself.
question: What lesson did Priya learn?

choices.text: Working together is better than scoring alone
It is best to keep the ball to yourself

explanation: The last sentence says Priya learned that working together was even better than scoring by herself.
hint: Read the very last sentence.

title: The Haunted Forest
passage:
Mila and her brother Theo took a shortcut through the old forest on the way home. Tall trees blocked out the sunlight, and a cold mist curled around their feet.
Suddenly, they heard a low moan and saw a glowing shape between the trees. Theo froze, but Mila stepped closer to look. The 'ghost' was really an old white sheet caught on a branch, flapping in the wind. The moaning sound came from the wind whistling through a hollow log.
The children laughed at how scared they had been. They walked home, telling everyone about the forest that only seemed haunted.
question: What did the glowing 'ghost' turn out to be?

choices.text: An old white sheet caught on a branch

explanation: The 'ghost' was really an old white sheet caught on a branch, flapping in the wind.
hint: Read the second paragraph where the mystery is explained.

title: The Haunted House on the Hill
passage:
At the top of the hill stood an old house that everyone called haunted. Its windows were dusty and the gate squeaked loudly.
One afternoon, brave young Sam decided to find out the truth. He pushed open the creaky gate and knocked on the door. To his surprise, a friendly old man named Mr Bell answered.
Mr Bell explained that he lived alone and could not fix the squeaky gate or clean the high windows. Sam offered to help. Soon the house looked bright and welcoming, and the neighbours stopped calling it haunted.
question: Why did everyone think the house was haunted?

choices.text: It had dusty windows and a squeaky gate

explanation: The house had dusty windows and a loudly squeaking gate, which made it seem haunted.
hint: Read the first paragraph describing the house.

title: Deep in the Ocean
passage:
Far below the waves, the ocean is full of amazing creatures. Near the top, where sunlight reaches, colourful fish dart between the coral.
Deeper down, it grows dark and cold. Some fish here make their own light to find food, glowing like tiny lanterns. Giant whales swim through these waters, singing songs that travel for many kilometres.
At the very bottom, strange animals live in the darkness. Scientists use special submarines to explore these deep places, where no sunlight has ever reached.
question: Why can colourful fish live near the top of the ocean?

choices.text: Because it is the coldest part
Because there is no water there

explanation: The text says near the top, sunlight reaches, and colourful fish dart between the coral.
hint: Read the first paragraph about the top of the ocean.

title: The Candy Thief
passage:
Each morning, Lucy found that lollies were missing from the jar on the kitchen bench. "Who is the candy thief?" she wondered.
Lucy decided to solve the mystery. That night, she hid behind the door and watched the jar. After a while, she heard a tiny scratching sound. It was not her little brother at all — it was a clever pet mouse named Squeak, climbing up to nibble the sweets!
Lucy gently moved the jar to a high shelf with a tight lid. The mystery was solved, and Squeak had to find his snacks somewhere else.
question: What was going missing from the jar?
explanation: The first paragraph says lollies were missing from the jar.
hint: Read the first sentence.

title: The Young Scientist
passage:
Aisha loved doing experiments in her garage. Her favourite was making a volcano that fizzed and bubbled.
She poured baking soda into a bottle shaped like a mountain. Then she added vinegar mixed with red food colouring. At once, the mixture foamed up and spilled over the top like red lava!
Aisha wrote down what happened in her science notebook. A good scientist, she knew, always records what they see. She could not wait to try a new experiment the next day.
question: What was Aisha's favourite experiment?
explanation: Her favourite experiment was making a volcano that fizzed and bubbled.
hint: Read the first paragraph.

title: A Visit to the Doctor
passage:
Noah had a sore throat, so his mum took him to see Dr Lee. Noah felt a little worried as he sat in the waiting room.
Dr Lee was kind and gentle. She used a small torch to look inside Noah's throat and listened to his chest with a stethoscope. "You have a mild infection," she said with a smile. "Lots of rest and water will help you feel better."
Noah felt much calmer. He realised that doctors are there to help us stay healthy, not to be scared of.
question: Why did Noah go to see Dr Lee?
explanation: The first sentence says Noah had a sore throat.
hint: Read the first paragraph.

title: Diwali in India
passage:
Anya was excited because it was time for Diwali, the festival of lights. Her family had travelled to visit her grandparents in India.
Every evening, they lit small clay lamps called diyas and placed them around the house. The little flames glowed warmly in the dark. Colourful patterns called rangoli were drawn on the floor using bright powders.
The whole street sparkled with lights, and families shared sweets called mithai. Anya thought it was the most beautiful celebration she had ever seen.
question: What festival is the story about?
explanation: The story is about Diwali, the festival of lights.
hint: Read the first sentence.
